root_text.sn47.30:1.2: Tena kho pana samayena mānadinno gahapati ābādhiko hoti dukkhito bāḷhagilāno.
root_text.sn47.30:1.3: Atha kho mānadinno gahapati aññataraṁ purisaṁ āmantesi:
root_text.sn47.30:1.5: na me, bhante, khamanīyaṁ na yāpanīyaṁ. Bāḷhā me dukkhā vedanā abhikkamanti, no paṭikkamanti; abhikkamosānaṁ paññāyati, no paṭikkamoti.
root_text.sn47.30:1.6: Evarūpāya cāhaṁ, bhante, dukkhāya vedanāya phuṭṭho samāno kāye kāyānupassī viharāmi ātāpī sampajāno satimā, vineyya loke abhijjhādomanassaṁ;
root_text.sn47.30:1.9: dhammesu dhammānupassī viharāmi ātāpī sampajāno satimā, vineyya loke abhijjhādomanassaṁ.
root_text.sn47.30:1.10: Yāni cimāni, bhante, bhagavatā pañcorambhāgiyāni saṁyojanāni desitāni, nāhaṁ, bhante, tesaṁ kiñci attani appahīnaṁ samanupassāmī”ti.

translation_text.sn47.30:1.2: Now at that time the householder Mānadinna was sick, suffering, gravely ill.
translation_text.sn47.30:1.4: “Please, my friend, go to Venerable Ānanda …” …
translation_text.sn47.30:1.5: “Sir, I’m not keeping well, I’m not getting by. The pain is terrible and growing, not fading; its growing is evident, not its fading.
translation_text.sn47.30:1.6: When I experience such painful feelings I meditate observing an aspect of the body—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world.
translation_text.sn47.30:1.7: I meditate observing an aspect of feelings …
translation_text.sn47.30:1.9: principles—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world.
translation_text.sn47.30:1.10: And of the five lower fetters taught by the Buddha, I don’t see any that I haven’t given up.”
translation_text.sn47.30:1.12: You have declared the fruit of non-return.”
